column7: message

By that logic anytime you try to resolve an issue with a person directly it would be considered a threat. For example trying to be an adult going to a coworker and saying hey I don't appreciate XYZ behavior I'd like to find a solution or I will have to go to HR. I just don't see how one is acceptable but CNN saying hey we find these attacks against us inappropriate we would like you to stop or you will need to face the social consequences of your own actions is wrong.

Well, it's definitely a threat.  

The network is saying that they will do something he clearly doesn't want to happen unless they do what ~~he~~ they say.  That's definitely a threat.  I don't think there's anything wrong with revealing his identity but they're still threatening him with it.  

EDIT: Typo

I guess it depends on how you look at it. If the network is just stating their policy and consequences for violating it, it might not seem like a direct threat. But if it feels intimidating or coercive to the individual, then maybe it crosses that line. It’s a tricky balance.

column7: Ironically, him assuming that I'd like mail order grenades because I oppose gun control is an example of a slippery slope.

Ultimately this is why:

>So we already have some sensible limitations on our right to bear arms, why not keep expanding them?

Gun owners are pretty thoroughly convinced that the limitations will only ever expand until they aren't allowed to own guns at all.  So they fight every limitation with every political tool they have available.

column7: And yet, there's every reason to believe that Poland would be more diverse now and it would have been in the Middle Ages. Its membership in the European Union and it's relatively strong economy, compared to many places in Africa, mean that it has great value for immigrants and people wanting to improve their lives. Are you seriously suggesting that Poland used to be more diverse, despite all of it in to the contrary, and for some reason all of the ethnic minorities just left within the past hundred years or so?

I think it has nothing to with the medium, just the framing. If you frame your game / movie / novel as a crazy alternative history spin on some time period or event (like Wolfenstein or Inglorious Basterds did), that's fine. But if you claim you try to adhere to historical accuracy, you can't just blatantly change parts of history just because they don't suit your worldview, because your work becomes part of the modern corpus describing the historical event or time period, and people expect to get a sense of what it was like coming into it.
"Agreed! It's important to maintain some level of historical accuracy, especially when presenting something as factual. Otherwise, it risks distorting our understanding of the past."
